A Health, Skills & Dignified-Ageing Campus · Chengalpattu, Tamil Nadu

An Integrated Nursing, Paramedic & Assisted-Living Campus

Not merely an education or real-estate project — a rural human-capital platform that converts demand for health workers and dignified eldercare into certified livelihoods for local youth and women, while building a dependable continuum of care for senior citizens.

Explore the project See the site & campus
FIG. 00 · CAMPUS RENDERING
₹35 CrEst. investment
5 lakhSq ft built-up · in phases
8.17Acres (total site)
1,500–2,500Livelihoods at maturity
Sheet 01Why this project matters

A campus that keeps healthcare careers close to home

Chengalpattu sits in the Chennai peri-urban corridor with a large rural hinterland — and two urgent, converging social needs.

A campus combining nursing education, paramedical training, hostels and assisted living can connect nearby villages to formal healthcare employment without forcing permanent migration to distant cities. It strengthens the region's healthcare workforce while giving families a reliable, dignity-centred option for the care of their elders.

A shortage of trained healthcare personnel

Tamil Nadu's hospitals and healthcare institutions need a steady supply of qualified nursing and paramedical professionals. This campus trains them locally.

A rising need for professional eldercare

Families increasingly require professional, dignity-centred support for senior citizens — a service that barely exists in the district today.

Public benefit at a glance
  • 01Affordable, quality nursing education for rural and economically weaker students.
  • 02Direct and indirect employment for local youth — teaching, administrative, technical and support roles.
  • 03More qualified nursing professionals to serve hospitals across Tamil Nadu.
  • 04Women's education, empowerment and financial independence through professional training.
  • 05Support for the Government's vision of stronger healthcare infrastructure and human resources.
  • 06Local economic development through infrastructure creation and new business for nearby communities.
The core idea

From an institutional campus to a regional development engine.

Sheet 02Socioeconomic impact

Five ways the campus creates value locally

Measurable, recurring benefit across employment, skills, women's participation, eldercare and rural enterprise.

Rural employment

Direct jobs in education, eldercare, hospital support, hostels and administration — plus indirect livelihoods in food, laundry, transport, maintenance, supplies and local services.

Upskilling

A skills ladder from bridge courses and short certificates to technical-assistant roles, formal nursing and paramedical programmes, specialty training and entrepreneurship.

Women empowerment

Safe hostel-based education, local hiring, SHG vendor contracts, women-friendly transport, grievance systems and pathways from informal care work to paid skilled work.

Elderly care economy

Assisted living, home-care training, chronic-disease support, rehabilitation, dementia-sensitive routines, family respite and caregiver education.

Local enterprise

Recurring procurement — vegetables, milk, uniforms, linen, laundry, housekeeping, transport, repairs, stationery and digital support — anchors rural micro-enterprises.

Sheet 03Employment generation

Impact arrives in three waves

Construction jobs, then recurring campus roles, then indirect local livelihoods. Figures are indicative and will be validated in the DPR and manpower budget.

PhaseEmployment channelsIndicative scale
EstablishmentPHASE 1Construction workers; civil, electrical and plumbing vendors; first faculty; hostel staff; kitchen, housekeeping and security; initial eldercare team.
300–600 temporary construction
150–300 recurring roles
Scale-upPHASE 2Expanded faculty and trainers; eldercare workers and rehab aides; placement team; transport, procurement and outreach.
350–700 recurring roles
600–1,200 total livelihoods
Mature campusPHASE 3Full education campus and simulation centre; assisted living; home-care network; research and a rural-enterprise ecosystem.
700–1,200 recurring direct roles
1,500–2,500 total livelihoods
Sheet 04Rural skilling & mobility

A ladder from the village to a nursing career

Rural learners are bridged into healthcare work, earn job-linked certificates, and can climb toward formal qualifications and enterprise.

Bridge foundation

Preparing rural learners before formal training:

  • Basic English and Tamil medical vocabulary
  • Digital literacy and communication
  • Infection-control basics
  • Patient dignity and care ethics

Job-linked certificates

Short, employable qualifications:

Patient Care AssistantGeriatric Care AssistantHome Health AideHospital Housekeeping & Infection ControlPhlebotomy / Sample LogisticsMedical Records / HIMSNutrition / Therapeutic Kitchen AideLaundry / Linen / CSSD
Career mobility ladder
Step 1Short courses
Step 2Technical-assistant roles
Step 3Formal nursing & paramedical
Step 4Specialty certification
Step 5SHG / micro-enterprise
Sheet 05Women-led development

Women as students, employees, vendors and entrepreneurs

Safe accommodation, transport, mentoring and transparent grievance systems are not welfare add-ons — they are the core enablers of women entering shift-based healthcare work.

InterventionExpected socioeconomic effect
Targeted inclusionMinimum 60% women participation in short healthcare-support courses after stabilisation.
Local recruitmentAt least 50% of non-specialist operational roles from Chengalpattu and nearby rural blocks.
SHG / vendor development10–15 active local vendor or SHG contracts in food, linen, laundry, supplies or campus services.
Income mobilityTrack wage uplift at 6 and 12 months; target 70%+ placement or self-employment linkage within 6 months.
60%
Women participation
50%
Local operational hiring
10–15
SHG / vendor contracts
70%+
Placement in 6 months
Sheet 06Assisted living

A dignity-centred eldercare economy

Positioned as a continuum of dignity, safety and health support — and, integrated with the college, a living learning laboratory where students learn empathy and practical care while elders receive supervised support.

Care services

  • Medication reminders
  • Assisted daily living
  • Fall-prevention design
  • Nursing observation
  • Emergency escalation

Health support

  • Physiotherapy & rehab
  • Chronic-disease monitoring
  • Nutrition & vaccination
  • Teleconsultation
  • Eye & dental screening

Jobs created

  • Geriatric care assistants
  • Home-health & rehab aides
  • Nutrition aides
  • Activity coordinators
  • Elder companions

Family benefit

  • Respite care
  • Caregiver training
  • Transparent care plans
  • Family meetings
  • Home-care coordination
Sheet 07The site & its setting

This location comes under three villages

The site lies along Ninnakarai Road in Chengalpattu Taluk and is part of three revenue villages — Kayarampadu, Potheri and Vallancheri, bordered by Reserved Forest.

Sheet 09The campus masterplan

A single site, purpose-planned for care and learning

Education, residence, eldercare and services organised across one integrated masterplan, with rooftop solar on the college block and generous landscaped open space. Hover or tap the markers to explore each block.

  • 1
    Admin BlockReception, administration and governance
  • 2
    Open Space ReserveLandscaped gardens and walking paths
  • 3
    Assisted Living BlockSupervised residential eldercare
  • 4
    ParkingVisitor and staff parking
  • 5
    Nurse Hostel 1Safe student accommodation
  • 6
    College BlockClassrooms & simulation · rooftop solar
  • 7
    Nurse Hostel 2Safe student accommodation
  • 8
    Service YardLogistics, utilities and support
Sheet 10Local procurement

Rural value chains, anchored by the campus

Local-first policy

A “local first where quality and compliance are met” approach to food, textiles, laundry, housekeeping, gardening, maintenance, transport and digital support.

Vendor development

Training for SHGs, farmer-producer organisations and youth entrepreneurs in hygiene, documentation, infection control, GST basics, billing and digital payments.

Tracked as KPIs

Local procurement value, vendor payment-cycle time and quality scores are reported as annual socioeconomic indicators.

Sheet 11Infrastructure & sustainability

Built to a green institutional standard

Long-term sustainability is designed into the campus — lowering environmental impact and long-term utility costs alike.

Rainwater harvesting

Captures and stores runoff for non-potable use, reducing dependence on municipal supply.

Sewage treatment

An onsite STP treats wastewater; treated water is reused for irrigation and landscaping, minimising discharge.

Solar power

Rooftop solar offsets a significant share of campus energy, cutting the carbon footprint and operating cost.

Solid-waste management

Source-segregation, composting of organic waste and tie-ups with authorised recyclers for dry waste.

Sheet 12Economic impact

A measurable contribution to the district economy

₹35 CrEstimated total investment across ~5 lakh sq ft of built-up area.
₹35 CrIndicative local-body tax to Chengalpattu Municipal / Panchayat over the project lifecycle.
1,500–2,500Direct and indirect livelihoods sustained at a mature campus.

Beyond direct tax contribution, the campus generates substantial direct employment — faculty, healthcare staff, administration, hostel and eldercare personnel — and indirect employment across construction, supply chains, local vendors, transport and services, injecting recurrent income into the local economy.

Procurement from local suppliers, SHG vendor contracts and the sustained spending of staff and resident families create a multiplier effect — supporting rural micro-enterprises and anchoring durable economic activity in Chengalpattu district.

What we commit to measure annually

Employment

  • Direct jobs and indirect livelihoods
  • Percentage of local hires; 12-month retention
  • Women employed and women supervisors
  • SHG contracts and wage uplift
  • Local procurement value and payment timelines

Upskilling

  • Number trained; completion and pass rates
  • Placement rate and median salary
  • Bridge-course completion; rural retention
  • Advanced-certification progression
  • Alumni tracking and entrepreneurship starts

Community & eldercare

  • Assisted-living occupancy and satisfaction
  • Fall rate; caregiver training hours
  • Screening camps and home-safety assessments
  • Referrals completed; caregiver sessions
  • Grievances resolved; safeguarding & compliance
Sheet 13Compliance & land status

Committed to full statutory compliance

All required approvals will be obtained prior to construction and operations, with ongoing compliance maintained through internal monitoring and periodic third-party audits.

Approvals to be secured

  • Environmental clearances
  • Building-plan sanctions
  • Fire and safety NOCs
  • Healthcare-facility licences
  • Educational-institution affiliations
  • Other central, state and local permits

Compliance & monitoring

Compliance is treated as an ongoing commitment, not a one-time clearance:

  • Adherence to Tamil Nadu, local-body and council norms
  • Dedicated internal compliance monitoring
  • Periodic third-party audits

In summary

A Health, Skills & Dignified-Ageing Campus

Sugah Chengalpattu is a replicable model showing how healthcare education, residential infrastructure and assisted living can generate inclusive economic development while meeting a genuine social need.
